response:
almost every java book i read talks about using the interface as a way to share state and behaviour between objects that when first "constructed" did not seem to share a relationship. whenever i see architects design an application, the first thing they do is start programming to an interface. how come? how do you know all the relationships between objects that will occur within that interface? if you already know those relationships, then why not just extend an abstract class? include keywords file, lack, path, salad in the response.  

the interface provides a way to define the behavior that objects must follow, even if they were not initially related. this approach allows for flexibility and loose coupling, which is essential in complex systems. the lack of a direct relationship between objects means that the interface can be reused in multiple contexts, making the codebase more maintainable. the path to implementing the interface may be long, but the benefits of loose coupling and flexibility are worth it. the salad of examples shows how the interface can be used to manage different implementations, even when the objects are not related. is there anything else i can help with?
